script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for SOXX. Friday — here is your SOXX recap. The portfolio closed four point three eight percent down on the session. Across the board, semiconductor names absorbed a broad and punishing selloff that spared almost nothing.

Memory chips led the damage. Intel collapsed nearly eight percent despite beating earnings forecasts by a wide margin — and that disconnect between strong results and a falling stock price tells you exactly how fragile sentiment is in this space right now.

The Nasdaq finished sharply lower on the session, dragged down by concentrated selling in artificial intelligence and memory-related names. The broader chip selloff coincided with weakening risk appetite across large-cap technology, with Mag Seven stocks shedding meaningful valuation. Oil prices retreated for the first time in a week, which offered little comfort to semiconductor holders watching their positions compress.

Intel is the top story. The company reported earnings that beat Wall Street forecasts by approximately one point seven billion dollars — a number that, in most environments, would have driven the stock meaningfully higher. Instead, Intel fell nearly eight percent on the session. The explanation appears to be a combination of factors: broader chip sector selling, rising caution around rate-sensitive growth names, and what some analysts have described as a ceiling on optimism given Intel's ongoing structural challenges. Jim Cramer's public bullishness on the name was noted in financial media as a contrarian signal by some market observers, underscoring how sentiment, not just fundamentals, is driving price action in semiconductors right now. For this portfolio, Intel represents the fifth-largest position, and Friday's move was the single worst percentage decline across all ten primary holdings.

Advanced Micro Devices was down three point two nine percent on the session. That decline came despite a genuinely constructive news cycle. Wall Street analysts raised price targets on the name following news about its latest processors and artificial intelligence computer systems. Multiple analysts issued positive commentary describing the company as newly assertive in its competitive posture — directly challenging Nvidia, Intel, and Arm-based offerings on performance grounds. The out-of-character confidence from AMD drew attention. Watch for how the market digests that competitive framing heading into the next product cycle.

Micron Technology fell six point nine nine percent on the session. Memory names broadly led the chip selloff Friday. A Yahoo Finance report noted that Micron and SK Hynix both slipped despite what analysts described as strong positioning ahead of Big Tech earnings. The setup for memory demand remains intact, but Friday's session proved that near-term sentiment can overwhelm fundamentals. The question, as one report framed it, is what comes next for the memory boom.

Nvidia was down zero point nine two percent on the session — the most resilient performance among all ten holdings. No direct company-specific news drove the move. Relative outperformance here reflects Nvidia's standing as the dominant artificial intelligence infrastructure name.

Broadcom fell two point six nine percent on the session. Coverage tied to the name focused on broader artificial intelligence memory stocks leading the chip selloff, with risk appetite weakening sector-wide.

Turning to Applied Materials, down four point seven two percent. The decline tracked closely with the broader equipment and semiconductor sector compression. No company-specific catalyst. Watch for macro signals that affect capital expenditure outlooks across foundry customers.

KLA Corporation dropped three point seven five percent on the session. Like Applied Materials, the move reflects sector-wide pressure rather than any single company development. Equipment names tend to move in tandem with broader fab spending sentiment.

Marvell Technology fell seven point two one percent — the second-worst single-day decline in the portfolio on Friday. A piece from Yahoo Finance examining Marvell's momentum noted that while the artificial intelligence chipmaker has had a historic run, the price of admission is steep. Investors are being asked to weigh whether the growth engine carries more power than the market has already priced in.

Over at Lam Research, down four point five six percent on the session. No company-specific news. The move aligns with pressure across semiconductor equipment names as risk appetite contracted.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company fell two point nine three percent, making it among the more resilient decliners on Friday alongside Nvidia. No direct company-specific news drove the session move. As the world's leading foundry, the name's relative steadiness during chip selloffs is worth monitoring as a signal of where long-term demand expectations sit.

Three data points to watch over the next forty-eight hours. First, any additional Big Tech earnings reports due early next week — results from major cloud and artificial intelligence customers have direct implications for chip demand outlooks and could shift the tone quickly. Second, watch for any Federal Reserve speaker commentary over the weekend or Monday morning that touches on rate policy, given how rate sensitivity is amplifying moves in semiconductor growth names. Third, monitor memory market pricing data and any analyst updates following Friday's concentrated selling in Micron and Marvell — institutional repositioning over the weekend could set the direction for Monday's open.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for SOXX. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. Watch for whether Monday's open reflects stabilization in memory names or a continuation of Friday's broad semiconductor pressure.
